profile.name: Tobi Awolaju
profile.title: Full-Stack Engineer (Web3) — I ship real-time systems solo, from contract to frontend.
profile.bio: I build the systems most people are afraid to touch: real-time multiplayer sync, reorg-resistant blockchain indexers, and smart contracts that hold user funds. Currently finishing a degree in Electrical & Electronics Engineering at Adeleke University while building full-stack Web3 products solo — from Solidity to WebSocket servers to React frontends. I own the whole stack because I've had to.

projects.title: World of Nads ↗
projects.description: 20-player browser battle royale — 3D assets, a 20Hz WebSocket server keeping every player in sync in real time, on-chain prize distribution, and embedded wallet onboarding. No install, runs in-browser.

writeup.hardPart: Keeping 20 players' state perfectly synced over WebSocket while the frontend also has to talk to a wallet layer and on-chain state — three moving pieces that all had to stay consistent under real network jitter. I profiled and fixed a bottleneck where per-frame calls across the Godot/React bridge were tanking browser FPS from 60 to under 12, then rebuilt the sync path around batched state updates.
writeup.stack: Godot (WASM), TypeScript, React, Node.js WebSocket server, Solidity, PostgreSQL, Privy.

projects.title: MoyakiTraceEngine ↗
projects.description: A block indexer built for chains that reorganize. Cross-references three independent Monad nodes in real time and automatically rolls back state the moment it detects a parent-hash mismatch, so downstream consumers never read bad data.

writeup.hardPart: Public RPCs disagree with each other more than people expect. I designed a ReorgManager that treats disagreement as the normal case, not the exception — comparing state across nodes continuously and rolling back cleanly rather than crashing or silently serving stale data.
writeup.stack: Node.js, Express, Svelte, Firebase RTDB, EVM RPCs.

projects.title: Rug Pool ↗
projects.description: A memecoin launchpad where no one — not even the deployer — can exit early. On-chain, verifiable randomness decides who exits at the 24-hour mark; losers fund the winner's payout.

writeup.hardPart: Enforcing a hard lock at the contract level with no admin backdoor, while making the randomness both unpredictable and independently verifiable. Used Pyth Entropy for VRF. Shipped 5 contracts to Monad testnet with 53 passing tests and ran two full simulation cycles to confirm the payout logic held under edge cases.
